content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

message.content: Read prompt.md and complete the exercise following the workflow rules.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll read the prompt file to understand the exercise.

content.content:
1	# MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2	
3	## Setting
4	
5	You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6	Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7	an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8	swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9	moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10	its venerable traditions.
11	
12	## Feature
13	
14	The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15	a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16	damage report against an existing policy.
17	
18	## Rules
19	
20	### Item values and base premiums
21	
22	Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23	the MHPCO price list:
24	
25	- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26	- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27	- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28	- Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29	
30	Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31	each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32	3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33	
34	### Premium modifiers
35	
36	- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37	- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38	  surcharge.
39	- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40	  20 % loyalty discount.
41	- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42	- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43	- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44	
45	All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46	
47	### Claim processing
48	
49	A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50	policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51	
52	- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53	  the damage amount.
54	- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55	
56	## Examples
57	
58	The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59	are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60	modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61	
62	### Building block of 3 alike components
63	
64	- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65	- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66	- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67	- 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68	
69	### "Alike" components
70	
71	> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72	> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73	
74	- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75	- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76	
77	### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78	
79	> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80	> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81	
82	- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83	  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84	  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85	  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86	- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87	  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88	  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89	  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90	  added at the very end
91	
92	### Modifier thresholds
93	
94	- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95	- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96	  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97	- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98	  surcharge applies only if cursed
99	- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100	  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101	
102	### Deductible per damage event
103	
104	- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105	  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106	  per damaged item)
107	
108	### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109	
110	- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111	  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112	- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113	  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114	  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115	
116	### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117	
118	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119	  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120	- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121	  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122	  then deductible: 800 − 100)
123	- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124	  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125	  deductible: 500 − 100)
126	
127	### Multiple items of the same type
128	
129	> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130	> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131	> two?
132	
133	- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134	  cap 4000 G
135	- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136	  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137	  separate damage with its own deductible
138	- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139	  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140	  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141	  whole claim is rejected
142	
143	### Cap exhaustion
144	
145	- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146	  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147	- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148	  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149	  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150	- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151	  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152	  only, not the insurance sum
153	- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154	  claims of 1500 G each
155	- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156	- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157	  is reduced to the remaining cap)
158	
159	### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160	
161	- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162	  (rounded up)
163	- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164	  (rounded down)
165	- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166	  only the final premium or payout is rounded
167	
168	### Edge cases
169	
170	- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171	- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172	  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173	  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174	  to stdout
175	- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176	  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177	  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178	  and writes an error description to stderr
179	- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180	  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181	  stderr
182	
183	## Integration examples
184	
185	The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186	modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187	
188	### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189	
190	- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192	- premium: 165 G
193	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194	
195	### Long-standing customer's second contract
196	
197	> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198	> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199	> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200	> insurance surcharge still apply?
201	
202	- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203	  in the scenario
204	- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205	- premium: 160 G
206	- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207	  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208	  = 160 G)
209	- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210	  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211	  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212	  history.
213	
214	## CLI input/output format
215	
216	The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217	`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218	scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219	stdout.
220	
221	A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222	array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223	scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224	operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225	later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226	step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227	
228	Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229	and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230	
231	- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232	- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233	  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234	  claim).
235	
236	### JSON Schema (normative)
237	
238	The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239	exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240	
241	**Input (stdin):**
242	
243	```json
244	{
245	  "type": "object",
246	  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247	  "properties": {
248	    "customer": {
249	      "type": "object",
250	      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251	      "properties": {
252	        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253	      }
254	    },
255	    "steps": {
256	      "type": "array",
257	      "items": {
258	        "oneOf": [
259	          {
260	            "description": "Quote step",
261	            "required": ["op", "items"],
262	            "properties": {
263	              "op": { "const": "quote" },
264	              "items": {
265	                "type": "array",
266	                "items": {
267	                  "type": "object",
268	                  "required": ["type"],
269	                  "properties": {
270	                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271	                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
272	                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273	                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274	                  }
275	                }
276	              }
277	            }
278	          },
279	          {
280	            "description": "Claim step",
281	            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282	            "properties": {
283	              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284	              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285	              "incident": {
286	                "type": "object",
287	                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288	                "properties": {
289	                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
290	                  "damages": {
291	                    "type": "array",
292	                    "items": {
293	                      "type": "object",
294	                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295	                      "properties": {
296	                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297	                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298	                      }
299	                    }
300	                  }
301	                }
302	              }
303	            }
304	          }
305	        ]
306	      }
307	    }
308	  }
309	}
310	```
311	
312	**Output (stdout):**
313	
314	```json
315	{
316	  "type": "object",
317	  "required": ["results"],
318	  "properties": {
319	    "results": {
320	      "type": "array",
321	      "items": {
322	        "oneOf": [
323	          {
324	            "description": "Quote result",
325	            "required": ["premium"],
326	            "properties": {
327	              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328	            }
329	          },
330	          {
331	            "description": "Claim result",
332	            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333	            "properties": {
334	              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335	              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336	            }
337	          }
338	        ]
339	      }
340	    }
341	  }
342	}
343	```
344	
345	### Schema example
346	
347	Stdin:
348	
349	```json
350	{
351	  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352	  "steps": [
353	    {
354	      "op": "quote",
355	      "items": [
356	        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357	      ]
358	    },
359	    {
360	      "op": "claim",
361	      "policy": 0,
362	      "incident": {
363	        "cause": "fire",
364	        "damages": [
365	          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366	        ]
367	      }
368	    }
369	  ]
370	}
371	```
372	
373	Stdout shape:
374	
375	```json
376	{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377	```
378	
379	## Expected Output Files
380	
381	- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382	- Test files
383	- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384	
385	## Constraints
386	
387	- Use TypeScript for the implementation
388	- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389	- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390	- Internal data structures and module organization are free
391

# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### JSON Schema (normative)

The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.

**Input (stdin):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["customer", "steps"],
  "properties": {
    "customer": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
      "properties": {
        "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
      }
    },
    "steps": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote step",
            "required": ["op", "items"],
            "properties": {
              "op": { "const": "quote" },
              "items": {
                "type": "array",
                "items": {
                  "type": "object",
                  "required": ["type"],
                  "properties": {
                    "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
                    "material":    { "type": "string" },
                    "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
                    "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim step",
            "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
            "properties": {
              "op":     { "const": "claim" },
              "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
              "incident": {
                "type": "object",
                "required": ["cause", "damages"],
                "properties": {
                  "cause": { "type": "string" },
                  "damages": {
                    "type": "array",
                    "items": {
                      "type": "object",
                      "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
                      "properties": {
                        "itemType": { "type": "string" },
                        "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
                      }
                    }
                  }
                }
              }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Output (stdout):**

```json
{
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["results"],
  "properties": {
    "results": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "oneOf": [
          {
            "description": "Quote result",
            "required": ["premium"],
            "properties": {
              "premium": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          },
          {
            "description": "Claim result",
            "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
            "properties": {
              "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
              "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
            }
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

- Claim: 100G deductible per damage, cap at 2x insurance sum
- Damage modifiers: enchantment ≥8 (50% payout), dragon material (full reimbursement)

- CLI input/output with customer + steps array

- Then deductible: 450.5 − 100 = 350.5
- Round DOWN: 350 ✓

For the "block discount does not lower insurance sum" test: sword + 3 runes:
- Insurance sum = 1000 + 3×250 = 1750
- Cap = 2× = 3500 ✓

content.text:
Tests pass. Let me now consider if any non-anticipatory improvement is possible.

**Possible micro-refactorings considered:**

1. **Tighten return type**: Change `unknown` to a more specific type like `{ results: Array<{ premium: number }> }`. 
   - Pros: Reveals intent better (Rule 2)
   - Cons: This would anticipate that ALL future scenarios return premium results. The next test (sword quote) will still return `premium`, but later tests for `claim` will return `{ payout, remainingCap }`. Adding a tight type now would either be wrong or would need to be a union — which anticipates.
   - Verdict: Anticipates future test shape. Don't do it.

2. **Rename `_scenario` to remove underscore**: The underscore convention indicates intentionally unused. Since it IS unused, the underscore is correct and reveals intent ("we know this is unused right now"). Keep it.

3. **Extract `5` to a named constant** like `PROCESSING_FEE`:
   - Pros: Reveals intent (Rule 2) — `5` is opaque
   - Cons: This anticipates the semantics from future tests. The test name mentions "only the processing fee", but the current implementation doesn't compute anything — it just returns a literal that happens to match. Extracting `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` would presuppose a decomposition (fee + base + ...) that no current test exercises. The current literal `5` matches the only behavior the test demands: "return this exact result for the empty case."
   - Verdict: Borderline anticipation. The constant name would lead toward a decomposition no current test demands.

4. **Inline the return into a single object literal** — already done.

**Step 6: Document Decision**

**Refactoring Evaluation**:
- Naming: `processScenario` already reveals intent at the right level of abstraction
- Duplication: No duplication exists (1-line body)
- Mass: Already minimal at 2
- Simplification: Cannot reduce — single return of a literal
- Type tightening: Would anticipate future return shapes (quote vs claim differ)
- Constant extraction: `PROCESSING_FEE = 5` would presuppose a decomposition the current test doesn't demand (the test just expects the exact value `5`, not a sum)

**Anticipation guard applied**: Any structural refactoring (lookup tables, dispatch on `_scenario.steps[0].op`, extracting pricing helpers) would make future `it.todo()` tests pass immediately when activated — breaking the Red phase. Rejected.

**Decision**: No refactoring performed. The current code is already at minimum mass (2), the name reveals intent, and any improvement I can think of would either anticipate future tests or sacrifice clarity. The hardcoded return is the textbook minimal Green-phase implementation and is appropriate to leave as-is at this stage of the TDD cycle.

**Step 7: Report**

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: None applied — code is already minimal and any change would anticipate future tests
- **Mass**: 2 (unchanged)
- **Tests**: All passing (1 active, 38 todo)

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

Refactored code:
```typescript
export function processScenario(scenario: unknown): unknown {
  const { steps } = scenario as Scenario;
  const results = steps.map((step) => {
    const items = step.items ?? [];
    if (items.length === 0) return { premium: 5 };
    if (items[0].type === "amulet") return { premium: 71 };
    return { premium: 115 };
  });
  return { results };
}
```

Component count:
- Constants: `5`, `71`, `115`, `0`, `"amulet"` = 5
- Bindings: `scenario`, `steps`, `results`, `step`, `items` = 5
- Invocations: `map` = 1 → 2
- Conditionals: 2 `if` + `??` = 3 → 12
- Loops: `map` = 1 → 5
- Assignments: 0

- Extracted `steps` via destructuring to separate the type assertion from the iteration logic
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Slightly improved — the cast `(scenario as Scenario)` is now isolated to one line, making the subsequent `.map` call cleaner and the typed nature of `steps` obvious
- Mass: 28 → 29 (Δ +1 from added binding) — clarity trumps APP per Rule 2 priority

content.text:
Now I'll evaluate the current implementation for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

- `processScenario`: Generic but matches the high-level intent (process a scenario with steps). Acceptable for now.
- `PROCESSING_FEE`: Clear, reveals intent.
- `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`: Clear lookup table name.
- `results`, `steps`, `items`, `step`: All clear in context.

Decision: Keep current names — they reveal intent well.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

```typescript
const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;
const PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE: Record<string, number> = {
  sword: 115, amulet: 71, staff: 93, potion: 49,
};

export function processScenario(scenario: unknown): unknown {
  const { steps } = scenario as Scenario;
  const results = steps.map((step) => {
    const items = step.items ?? [];
    if (items.length === 0) return { premium: PROCESSING_FEE };
    return { premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[items[0].type] };
  });
  return { results };
}
```

Component count (function body + module constants used):
- Constants: `5`, `115`, `71`, `93`, `49`, `0` (length check) = 6
- Bindings: `PROCESSING_FEE`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `steps`, `results`, `step`, `items` = 6
- Invocations: `map`, `??`, `items.length`, `items[0]`, `.type`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[...]` ≈ 6 → mass 12
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) = 4
- Loops: 1 (`map`) = 5
- Assignments: 0

Total Mass ≈ 6 + 6 + 12 + 4 + 5 + 0 = **33**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** 5/5 passing.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The branch `if (items.length === 0) return { premium: PROCESSING_FEE }` has a subtle meaning that could be clearer. However, there's a question of anticipation here — is the empty-items behavior really a separate case, or is it just that the lookup produces undefined?

Looking at the test: "empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)". The processing fee is part of every premium (e.g. sword = 100 + 10 + **5 fee**). So actually all premiums in the lookup table already include the 5 fee baked in. This is current behavior — no need to change.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No duplication present.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Could we eliminate the conditional? If we add an empty-items case to the lookup, we could remove the branch. But that would require a sentinel key or pre-checking the length anyway. The conditional is the simplest way to handle "no items".

Actually, looking carefully: the conditional handles two distinct conceptual cases (empty list vs. has items). Removing it would require either a different data shape or anticipating future structure. Keep it.

## Step 4: Anticipation Check

Checking remaining `it.todo()` entries: rune (33), 2 runes (60), multiple runes with blocks, enchantments, curse, loyalty, multi-item policies, etc. These all require computing premiums from components (base + first-insurance + fee + modifiers), not just lookup.

The current lookup-with-precomputed-totals approach is correct for today's 5 tests. It does NOT anticipate. Decomposing into `base + firstInsurance + fee` right now would anticipate the rune/enchantment/curse/loyalty tests.

## Step 5: Small Improvement — Extract `item` accessor for clarity

One minor clarity improvement: `items[0].type` reads a bit awkwardly. Extracting an explaining variable would slightly increase mass but improve Rule 2 (Reveals Intent). However, the current line is short and clear, so this would not be a meaningful improvement.

Let me consider another small change: the `step.items ?? []` defensive default exists, but every current test passes `items` explicitly. Removing the `?? []` would simplify, but might anticipate a future behavior decision. Keep it — it's defensive and minimal.

## Step 6: Try one refactoring — Tighten the empty-list handling

Actually, on closer reading, the empty-items handling and the lookup can be unified by treating "no item" as returning just the fee, but the conditional is exactly that. Let me try removing the unnecessary `step` destructuring intermediate.

Looking again, there's nothing to extract that wouldn't anticipate. Let me consider: is `const items = step.items ?? []` adding value? Without it: `const items = step.items` would need `items?.length` checks. The default keeps the rest simple. Keep it.

## Step 7: Attempt a micro-refactoring

Let me try extracting the per-step computation to a named function to better reveal intent — but with only one call site and a small body, that risks adding elements (Rule 4) without enough clarity gain.

One genuine improvement: in `if (items.length === 0) return { premium: PROCESSING_FEE }`, the `PROCESSING_FEE` here is exactly the premium when there are no items. The intent is clear. But notice that `items.length === 0` is just `!items.length` — same mass, but `=== 0` is clearer. Keep as is.

After careful evaluation, I'll attempt one small change: rename `step` parameter to be more specific. Actually `step` is fine — each entry in `steps` is a step.

## Step 8: Honest Assessment — No Refactoring Performed

After thorough evaluation against all four Simple Design Rules:

**Refactoring Evaluation**:
- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: 5/5 passing — no fix needed.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: All names clearly reveal intent (`processScenario`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `steps`, `items`, `results`).
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication exists — each constant appears once.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Current implementation has the minimum elements needed: one lookup table, one map, one branch for the no-items case.

**Anticipation Risks Avoided**:
- Did NOT decompose `115` into `100 + 10 + 5` (would anticipate enchantment/curse/loyalty tests).
- Did NOT extract a `computePremium(items, customer)` helper (no current test needs the parameters separated).

content.text:
Now I'll evaluate the current implementation for refactoring opportunities.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation

- `processScenario`: Generic but matches the high-level intent (process a scenario with steps). Acceptable for now.
- `PROCESSING_FEE`: Clear, reveals intent.
- `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`: Clear lookup table name.
- `results`, `steps`, `items`, `step`: All clear in context.

Decision: Keep current names — they reveal intent well.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

```typescript
const PROCESSING_FEE = 5;
const PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE: Record<string, number> = {
  sword: 115, amulet: 71, staff: 93, potion: 49,
};

export function processScenario(scenario: unknown): unknown {
  const { steps } = scenario as Scenario;
  const results = steps.map((step) => {
    const items = step.items ?? [];
    if (items.length === 0) return { premium: PROCESSING_FEE };
    return { premium: PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[items[0].type] };
  });
  return { results };
}
```

Component count (function body + module constants used):
- Constants: `5`, `115`, `71`, `93`, `49`, `0` (length check) = 6
- Bindings: `PROCESSING_FEE`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `steps`, `results`, `step`, `items` = 6
- Invocations: `map`, `??`, `items.length`, `items[0]`, `.type`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[...]` ≈ 6 → mass 12
- Conditionals: 1 (`if`) = 4
- Loops: 1 (`map`) = 5
- Assignments: 0

Total Mass ≈ 6 + 6 + 12 + 4 + 5 + 0 = **33**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** 5/5 passing.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The branch `if (items.length === 0) return { premium: PROCESSING_FEE }` has a subtle meaning that could be clearer. However, there's a question of anticipation here — is the empty-items behavior really a separate case, or is it just that the lookup produces undefined?

Looking at the test: "empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)". The processing fee is part of every premium (e.g. sword = 100 + 10 + **5 fee**). So actually all premiums in the lookup table already include the 5 fee baked in. This is current behavior — no need to change.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** No duplication present.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** Could we eliminate the conditional? If we add an empty-items case to the lookup, we could remove the branch. But that would require a sentinel key or pre-checking the length anyway. The conditional is the simplest way to handle "no items".

Actually, looking carefully: the conditional handles two distinct conceptual cases (empty list vs. has items). Removing it would require either a different data shape or anticipating future structure. Keep it.

## Step 4: Anticipation Check

Checking remaining `it.todo()` entries: rune (33), 2 runes (60), multiple runes with blocks, enchantments, curse, loyalty, multi-item policies, etc. These all require computing premiums from components (base + first-insurance + fee + modifiers), not just lookup.

The current lookup-with-precomputed-totals approach is correct for today's 5 tests. It does NOT anticipate. Decomposing into `base + firstInsurance + fee` right now would anticipate the rune/enchantment/curse/loyalty tests.

## Step 5: Small Improvement — Extract `item` accessor for clarity

One minor clarity improvement: `items[0].type` reads a bit awkwardly. Extracting an explaining variable would slightly increase mass but improve Rule 2 (Reveals Intent). However, the current line is short and clear, so this would not be a meaningful improvement.

Let me consider another small change: the `step.items ?? []` defensive default exists, but every current test passes `items` explicitly. Removing the `?? []` would simplify, but might anticipate a future behavior decision. Keep it — it's defensive and minimal.

## Step 6: Try one refactoring — Tighten the empty-list handling

Actually, on closer reading, the empty-items handling and the lookup can be unified by treating "no item" as returning just the fee, but the conditional is exactly that. Let me try removing the unnecessary `step` destructuring intermediate.

Looking again, there's nothing to extract that wouldn't anticipate. Let me consider: is `const items = step.items ?? []` adding value? Without it: `const items = step.items` would need `items?.length` checks. The default keeps the rest simple. Keep it.

## Step 7: Attempt a micro-refactoring

Let me try extracting the per-step computation to a named function to better reveal intent — but with only one call site and a small body, that risks adding elements (Rule 4) without enough clarity gain.

One genuine improvement: in `if (items.length === 0) return { premium: PROCESSING_FEE }`, the `PROCESSING_FEE` here is exactly the premium when there are no items. The intent is clear. But notice that `items.length === 0` is just `!items.length` — same mass, but `=== 0` is clearer. Keep as is.

After careful evaluation, I'll attempt one small change: rename `step` parameter to be more specific. Actually `step` is fine — each entry in `steps` is a step.

## Step 8: Honest Assessment — No Refactoring Performed

After thorough evaluation against all four Simple Design Rules:

**Refactoring Evaluation**:
- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: 5/5 passing — no fix needed.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: All names clearly reveal intent (`processScenario`, `PROCESSING_FEE`, `PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, `steps`, `items`, `results`).
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No duplication exists — each constant appears once.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Current implementation has the minimum elements needed: one lookup table, one map, one branch for the no-items case.

**Anticipation Risks Avoided**:
- Did NOT decompose `115` into `100 + 10 + 5` (would anticipate enchantment/curse/loyalty tests).
- Did NOT extract a `computePremium(items, customer)` helper (no current test needs the parameters separated).
- Did NOT add rune-block logic or quantity handling (anticipates rune tests).
- Did NOT add a `processStep` helper function — only one inline map step exists; extracting adds an element without payoff.

**Mass**: ~33 (unchanged — no refactoring performed).

**Reasoning for No Change**: The implementation is the minimal honest expression of the 5 currently-active tests. Any decomposition (e.g., splitting fee from base price, parameterizing by enchantment, introducing modifier pipelines) would immediately satisfy or partially satisfy `it.todo()` tests that have not yet been activated, violating the no-anticipation rule. The current lookup-table approach is the smallest design that fits today's contract and will need to grow when the next Red phase introduces a test the lookup cannot answer (e.g., the rune test, which needs quantity-based pricing).

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring**: None performed — already at the minimum honest design for the current test contract.
**Mass Change**: 33 → 33 (unchanged).
**Tests**: All 5 passing.

content.text:
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: Replaced the per-item-type hardcoded total premium lookup with a per-item base premium lookup; sum the base premiums across all items, add 10% first-insurance via `base/10` (avoids 0.1 float artifacts), ceil, then add the 5 G processing fee. Empty items still returns just the 5 G fee.
**Result**: All 7 active tests pass (32 still todo).
**Approach**: This is the simplest generalization needed to handle multiple items of the same type. The single-item tests still pass because the formula `ceil(base + base/10) + 5` reproduces all five hardcoded values (115/71/93/49/33). Using integer division by 10 instead of multiplying by 1.1 was necessary to avoid floating-point overshoot (e.g. `100*1.1 = 110.0000000001`). No future features added — block-of-3, modifiers, and rounding-down for payouts are still untouched.

content.text:
The journal exists — but per the agent rules above, the refactor agent reads only test files and current implementation source. The journal is written by red/green only. I'll skip touching it.

**Step 5: New APP Mass**

The refactored block:
```
const items = step.items ?? [];                  // 1 binding, 1 conditional = 5
const policyBase = items.reduce(...0);           // 1 binding, 1 invocation, callback (2 bindings, 2 invocations), 1 const = 9
const firstInsurance = policyBase / 10;          // 1 binding, 1 invocation (/), 1 const = 4
const premium = Math.ceil(policyBase + firstInsurance) + PROCESSING_FEE; // 1 binding, 3 invocations = 7
```

Removed: the entire conditional `items.length === 0 ? ... : ...` (1 conditional = 4, 1 invocation `===` = 2, 1 constant `0` = 1, plus the duplicated `+ PROCESSING_FEE`). Net savings ~6-7 mass units.

**Step 6: Refactoring Decision**

Refactoring applied:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Renamed `base` → `policyBase` (clearer that it represents the aggregated policy base). Introduced `firstInsurance` explaining variable making the 10% surcharge formula self-documenting (the test descriptions explicitly mention "first-insurance").
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Removed the unnecessary `items.length === 0 ? PROCESSING_FEE : ...` conditional — both branches produced identical results when items is empty (since `Math.ceil(0 + 0/10) + 5 === 5`). One conditional (mass 4) and one `===` invocation eliminated.
- APP Mass: reduced (~50 → ~44, Δ -6).

Not anticipating future tests: the refactoring did not add helpers, branches, lookup entries, or generalizations for upcoming behaviors (block discounts, enchantment surcharges, curse, loyalty, follow-up). The change purely simplifies and renames what currently-passing tests already exercise.

**Step 7: Refactor Phase Complete**

Refactoring: removed redundant empty-items conditional; renamed `base` → `policyBase`; extracted `firstInsurance` explaining variable.
Mass change: ~50 → ~44 (Δ -6).
Tests: 7 passing.

content.text:
All 11 tests still pass.

**Refactor Phase Complete**

**Naming Evaluation**: All existing names (`processScenario`, `policyBase`, `firstInsurance`, `countsByType`, `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE`, etc.) already reveal intent and match the domain language used in test descriptions. No renames needed. Added one new name: `quotePremium`, which names the extracted concern clearly.

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted the per-step premium calculation into a `quotePremium(items)` helper.

- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: The outer `processScenario` now reads as "for each step, compute its premium" — a one-liner that mirrors the scenario-step domain. The inner pricing rules (count by type, rune block of 3, first-insurance fee, processing fee) live in a dedicated function whose name announces what it does.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No new duplication.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: One extra named function, but the win in clarity outweighs the extra element. The mapping callback collapses from ~17 lines to 3.

**No Anticipation Check**: `quotePremium` only does what current passing tests demand. It does not branch by step `op`, does not handle claims, does not generalize the rune-block rule to other item types (which the next todo test "3 runes + 3 moonstones" would require — that test will need to fail in Red before any generalization is allowed). The rune block remains hardcoded to `type === "rune"`.

**Mass Change** (approximate):
- Before: ~38 (1 function, 1 map callback with ~17 lines of body)
- After: ~40 (2 functions, one trivial map callback)
- Slight mass increase (~+2) from the extra function signature and parameter typing, but Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP mass.

**Tests**: 11 passed, 28 todo. All green.

The refactor work doesn't warrant additional task tracking — it's a sub-step of test #12 within task #1. Proceeding with the report.

## Refactor Phase Complete

### Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
- `quotePremium`, `processScenario`, `countsByType`, `policyBase`, `firstInsurance`: all clearly reveal intent. Kept.
- `RUNE_BLOCK_OF_3_PRICE`: misleading after test 12 added moonstone block support. The price is no longer rune-specific.
  - **Renamed** → `BLOCK_OF_3_PRICE`

### Refactoring Applied
1. **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)** — Renamed `RUNE_BLOCK_OF_3_PRICE` to `BLOCK_OF_3_PRICE`. The price applies to any block-eligible type, not just runes.
2. **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)** — Extracted `BLOCK_ELIGIBLE_TYPES = new Set(["rune", "moonstone"])`. The previous `type === "rune" || type === "moonstone"` open-coded the concept; the named set states *why* these types branch into the block-of-3 path.

### Non-Anticipation Check
- The `BLOCK_ELIGIBLE_TYPES` set contains exactly the two types proven by current tests (rune, moonstone). No speculative types added.
- The block size is still hardcoded to `=== 3`. No generalization to other block sizes.
- The block price is still hardcoded to `60`. No generalization.
- The 10% first-insurance, ceiling, and processing fee logic untouched. No prep for cursed/enchantment/loyalty/follow-up tests still in `it.todo()`.

### APP Mass
- **Before**: ~78 (rough estimate)
  - condition `(type === "rune" || type === "moonstone") && count === 3` = 3 invocations + 1 conditional + 3 constants
- **After**: ~76 (rough estimate)
  - condition `BLOCK_ELIGIBLE_TYPES.has(type) && count === 3` = 2 invocations + 1 conditional + 1 constant + 1 binding lookup
- Mass slightly reduced; the primary win is Rule 2 (intent), which trumps mass.

### Tests
All 12 tests still passing; 27 `it.todo()` remain.

content.text:
Task list is for the orchestrator, not refactor agent. Skipping.

**APP Mass — Refactored `quotePremium`:**

Mass is essentially unchanged. The literal `5` became a binding reference `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` (constant→binding, both mass 1). The literal `0.3` became a binding reference (constant→binding, both mass 1). Net mass delta: 0.

**Refactoring Summary:**

- Rule 1 (Tests pass): Verified — 14/14 passing before and after.
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): Applied — extracted magic numbers `5` and `0.3` into named constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`, matching the naming pattern already established for `BLOCK_OF_3_PRICE`, `BLOCK_ELIGIBLE_TYPES`, `PROCESSING_FEE`. Reader no longer has to look at tests to understand what `0.3` and `5` mean.
- Rule 3 (No duplication): No actionable duplication. Two passes over `items` exist (one for type counts driving block discounts, one for per-item surcharges), but the two passes compute structurally different things at different granularities (type-level vs. item-level). Merging would entangle them.
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): Two new constants added, but they serve Rule 2 (clarity), which trumps Rule 4. Did not introduce any new function, branch, table entry, or generalization for future not-yet-active tests (cursed, loyalty, follow-up, claim payouts). No anticipation.

**APP Mass:** ~95 → ~95 (Δ 0). Clarity improved, mass preserved.

**Tests:** 14 passing, 25 todo. All green.

Refactor Phase Complete. Ready for the next Red phase.

content.text:
All 15 tests still pass.

## Refactoring Summary

**Naming Evaluation**: All names already reveal intent clearly. No renames.

**Refactoring Applied** (Rule 3 — remove duplication):
- Extracted a per-item `rate` accumulator so `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[item.type]` is referenced only once per item instead of duplicated across each surcharge branch.
- The shape of the code now makes additive surcharges explicit: each applicable rate adds to one accumulator, then multiplies the base premium once.

**Mass Change** (surcharge loop only, before → after):
- Before: 2 conditionals + 2 multiplications + 2 assignments + 2 lookups of `BASE_PREMIUM_BY_ITEM_TYPE[item.type]` = ~31
- After: 1 binding (rate) + 2 conditionals + 2 assignments + 1 multiplication + 1 lookup + 1 final assignment = ~27
- Modest mass reduction, clearer additive structure.

**Anticipation Check**: This refactoring does NOT enable any not-yet-active `it.todo()` test. The behavior is identical — surcharges are summed exactly as before. The next test (`loyalty discount`) requires customer info that isn't even threaded through `quotePremium` yet; this refactoring does nothing to ease that work prematurely.

**Other potential refactorings considered but rejected**:
- Merging the two passes over `items` (count loop + surcharge loop): would obscure the two distinct concerns (block pricing vs. per-item surcharges). Rule 2 (clarity) trumps Rule 4 (fewer elements).
- Extracting a `surchargeRateFor(item)` helper: adds an element without revealing more intent at the only call site. Defer until a second caller appears.

Refactor Phase Complete:
- Refactoring: extracted per-item surcharge rate accumulator (deduplicated base-premium lookup)
- Tests: 15 passing

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The change replaced division-by-literal-10 with multiplication by a named constant:
- Removed: 1 constant (`10`) + 1 invocation (`/`)
- Added: 1 binding reference (`FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE`) + 1 invocation (`*`)

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Extracted magic number `10` (the first-insurance divisor) to a named constant `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1`, and converted `policyBase / 10` to `policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` for consistency with how other rates (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`, `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`) are expressed.
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 16 tests still green.
- APP Mass: Essentially unchanged (~139).
- Naming: Existing function and variable names already reveal intent — kept as-is.
- Duplication: The two surcharge `if`-blocks could be table-driven, but with only two surcharges this would add elements without clarity benefit and would risk anticipating future surcharge variations (forbidden — no current test demands a 3rd surcharge).
- Fewest Elements: Did not add helper functions or abstractions — keeping the implementation flat matches what the current tests demand.

**Benefits**:
- Premium-affecting numeric policy now expressed uniformly as named "rate" constants.
- Removes the lone magic number `10` from the calculation body.
- No anticipation: the change only affects already-tested behavior (first-insurance surcharge appears in every passing test).

## Step 7: Report Completion

Refactor Phase Complete.

- **Refactoring**: Extracted magic number `10` to `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` constant; expressed first-insurance as `policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` for parallel structure with other rate constants.
- **Mass Change**: ~139 → ~139 (no net change; intent improved without complexity cost).
- **Tests**: 16 passing, 23 todo. All green.

- No special handling yet for enchantment/dragon material — not demanded by this test

content.text:
All 20 tests still pass.

## Step 6: Calculate New APP Mass (post-refactor for processScenario only)

**Before — processScenario body** (rough count of the dispatch + inline claim logic):
- Constants/literals: ~3
- Bindings: ~15 (lots of local variables for claim logic)
- Invocations: ~8
- Conditionals: ~3
- Loops: 1
- Assignments: ~3
- Approx mass: 15 + 16 + 12 + 5 + 9 = ~75 (rough)

**After — processScenario body**:
- Bindings: ~6 (customer, steps, yearsWithMHPCO, remainingCapByPolicy, quoteIndex, premium, step, results)
- Invocations: ~3 (steps.map, processClaim, quotePremium)
- Conditionals: 2 (op === "claim", ?? default)
- Loops: 1
- Assignments: 1 (quoteIndex += 1)
- Approx mass: ~6 + 6 + 8 + 5 + 6 = ~31

**Total system mass** (processScenario + new processClaim) ≈ 31 + ~45 = ~76 — roughly equivalent total mass, but **distributed cleanly across two single-purpose functions**, dramatically improving Rule 2 (Reveals Intent).

## Step 7: Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Extracted inline claim handling into a dedicated `processClaim` function
- `processScenario` is now a pure dispatcher: step-by-step it either claims or quotes
- `processClaim` owns all cap-tracking and damage-payout logic
- Removed the unused `index` parameter from the map callback
- Renamed `policyStep`/`items` (which shadowed nothing intentionally) to `policyItems` and introduced `policyIndex` for clarity over repeated `step.policy!`

**Simple Design Rule wins:**
- Rule 1 (Tests Pass): All 20 active tests still green
- Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): Each function now has a single clear purpose
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No regression
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): One additional function, but each function's local element count dropped substantially

**Mass Change:** processScenario body went from ~75 to ~31 (significantly clearer). Total mass roughly preserved, but better organized.

**Anticipation check:** The extraction restructured existing code only. None of the pending `it.todo()` items (dragon material, high-enchantment 50% rule, cap exhaustion across claims, per-item-type damage limits, CLI handling) is pre-implemented or made trivially passable by this refactor.

**Tests:** 20 passing, 19 todo — All passing.

All 32 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```typescript
import { processScenario } from "./claim-office.js";

let input = "";
process.stdin.setEncoding("utf8");
process.stdin.on("data", (chunk) => {
  input += chunk;
});
process.stdin.on("end", () => {
  const scenario = JSON.parse(input);
  process.stdout.write(JSON.stringify(processScenario(scenario)));
});
```

Component count:
- Constants: `""`, `"utf8"`, `"data"`, `"end"` = 4
- Bindings: `input`, `chunk`, `scenario` = 3 (removed `result`)
- Invocations: `setEncoding`, `on("data"...)`, `on("end"...)`, `JSON.parse`, `processScenario`, `process.stdout.write`, `JSON.stringify`, `+=` = 8 → 16
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: `input += chunk` = 1 → 6

**Total Mass: 4 + 3 + 16 + 0 + 0 + 6 = 29**

**Mass change: 30 → 29 (Δ -1)**

## Step 6: Decision Documentation

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): Removed the intermediate `const result` binding by inlining `processScenario(scenario)` directly into the `JSON.stringify(...)` argument
- Mass reduced from 30 to 29 (Δ -1, one fewer binding)
- Kept `const scenario` because it names the meaningful domain object (the parsed input is a scenario); inlining it further would force the line to read inside-out and hurt Rule 2 clarity

**Naming evaluation**: All identifiers (`input`, `chunk`, `scenario`) clearly reveal intent. No renaming needed.

**Anticipation check**: Did NOT add try/catch, stderr output, or non-zero exit handling — those behaviors are demanded by `it.todo()` entries (validation of unknown item types, schema errors, etc.) and adding them now would make future Red phases pass immediately. The current implementation does exactly what the one active CLI test requires: read stdin JSON → write stdout JSON → exit 0.

**Things deliberately NOT done**:
- Did not refactor `claim-office.ts` — only `cli.ts` was changed in the recent Green phase; the rest is already covered by prior refactor cycles
- Did not switch to `for await` async iterator — would add a loop (mass +5) for no clarity gain
- Did not extract a `readStdin()` helper — premature abstraction for a 12-line file

## Step 7: Report

Refactor Phase Complete:
- **Refactoring**: Removed intermediate `result` binding in `cli.ts` end handler; inlined `processScenario(scenario)` into the `JSON.stringify(...)` call
- **Mass Change**: 30 → 29 (Δ -1)
- **Tests**: All 32 passing, 7 todo remaining

- **Result**: All 36 tests now pass (3 still `it.todo`).
- **Approach**: Minimal — single membership check via `in` operator on the existing lookup table, and a try/catch around the existing CLI body. No new lookups, no new constants, no extra error classification.

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**:
- `quotePremium`, `processClaim`, `processScenario`, `adjustForEnchantment` — all clearly describe their purposes.
- Inside `quotePremium`: variables `loyaltyDiscount` and `followUpDiscount` are clearly named as monetary amounts. The variable `firstInsurance` did NOT follow this convention — its name sounded like a flag or condition rather than a charge amount.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)** — Applied:
- Renamed `firstInsurance` → `firstInsuranceCharge` for naming parallelism with `loyaltyDiscount`/`followUpDiscount` (all three are amounts added/subtracted in the final sum).

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No actionable duplication. The two passes over `items` in `quotePremium` (counting for block-pricing vs. surcharge accumulation) have distinct purposes; unifying them would couple unrelated concerns.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Already minimal. Considered extracting an `assertKnownItemType` helper but it would add an invocation per item (+2 each) and a function declaration for one line — net negative on APP without clarity gain.

**APP Mass — `quotePremium`**:
- Before: rename does not change structure → mass unchanged
- After: same structural mass; clarity slightly improved

**Anticipation Check**: The three `it.todo` entries are CLI error cases (unknown item type in claim, negative damage, over-insured damage count). The refactor did NOT add any branches, helpers, or validation for them. The unknown-type guard remains scoped to `quotePremium` as it already was.

**Considered and rejected**:
- Replacing `(err as Error).message` in cli.ts with `instanceof Error`: would ADD a conditional (+4 mass) and a branch for a case no current test exercises — anticipation risk.
- Extracting validation pass: would add a loop (+5 mass) for marginal clarity gain.
- Unifying the two `items` loops: would couple distinct concerns and hurt Rule 2.

**Tests**: 36 passing, 3 todo.

The journal is managed by red/green per the project instructions ("ONE line per phase (`N R | ...` for Red, `N G | ...` for Green)"), and the refactor agent reads only test files and current implementation. I'll skip the journal write — refactor isn't listed as a journal author in the shared-context protocol.

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `quotePremium`, `processClaim`, `adjustForEnchantment`, `processScenario` — all clearly reveal intent. No renames.
- New helper `initialCapFor` — name describes precisely what it computes for a policy's items.

**Refactoring Applied** (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):
Extracted the lazy cap-initialization expression in `processClaim` into a named helper `initialCapFor(policyItems)`. The call site is now:
```typescript
if (!(policyIndex in remainingCapByPolicy)) {
  remainingCapByPolicy[policyIndex] = initialCapFor(policyItems);
}
```
The intent of `processClaim` is no longer interrupted by an inline reduce; the dictionary lazy-init reads at a single level of abstraction.

**Anticipation Check**: The two open `it.todo`s concern damage validation (negative amount, more damages than insured). The new helper introduces no branches, no per-damage logic, and no per-type counting — it only sums insurance values, identical to before. No future test is silently activated.

**APP Mass — comparative for the changed region**:
- Inline lazy-init block (before): ~27
- Lazy-init call site (after): ~14
- New helper body adds ~12 elsewhere
- Net mass roughly unchanged; the gain is in clarity (Rule 2 over APP, as designed)

**Tests**: 37 passed, 2 todo. All green.

---

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Naming Evaluation**:
- `quotePremium`, `processClaim`, `processScenario`, `adjustForEnchantment`, `initialCapFor` — all clearly reveal intent. No renames needed.
- New helper named `reimbursementFor(damage, policyItems)` — describes what it returns (the reimbursement for one damage).

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted `reimbursementFor(damage, policyItems)` helper from the `damages.reduce` callback in `processClaim`.
- The reduce callback is now a one-liner that simply sums per-damage reimbursements.

**Simple Design Rules Applied**:
- Rule 1 (Tests pass): All 38 tests still green.
- Rule 2 (Reveals intent): The reduce now reads as "sum the reimbursement of each damage" — separating "what to reimburse for one damage" (validation + adjustment + deductible) from "how to combine across damages" (sum).
- Rule 3 (No duplication): No duplication introduced or present.
- Rule 4 (Fewest elements): One new function added — justified by the clarity gain in Rule 2.

**APP Mass (rough estimates for the affected region)**:
- Before (inline reduce callback, ~7 lines of logic): ~44 mass units (1 loop + 2 conditionals + ~10 invocations + 6 bindings + several constants)
- After: callback is `~2 invocations + 2 bindings` (≈ 7) plus the extracted `reimbursementFor` (~32: 2 conditionals + ~7 invocations + 4 bindings + 5 constants + the throws). Total ~39.
- Mass change: ~44 → ~39 (Δ -5), with significantly improved separation of concerns. Even if mass were equal, Rule 2 (clarity) justifies the change.

**No anticipation check**: The remaining `it.todo` ("more entries of a type than insured...") would require counting per-type insured items and rejecting the whole claim — that is not implemented here. The extraction preserves exactly the current behavior.

**Tests**: 38 passed, 1 todo — all green.
